ground_truth_fix: TL;DR: You should add direct-action flag to the tc filter command, as in Because eBPF is kind of more flexible than the traditional actions and filters of tc, you can actually do both at once, filter a packet (i.e. identify this packet) and perform an action on it. To reflect this flexibility, the direct-action, or da flag was added (for kernel 4.4 or newer, with matching iproute2 package). It tells the kernel to use the return values of actions (TC_ACT_SHOT, TC_ACT_OK, etc.) for classifiers. And this is what you need here to return TC_ACT_SHOT in a way the kernel understands you want to dr...

prompt_a:
Here is eBPF code that fails verification. Fix it.

Code:
SEC("simple")
int handle_ingress(struct __sk_buff *skb)
{
return TC_ACT_SHOT;
}
So I want ANY packets to be dropped. I install it as follows:
This happens on Ubuntu 16.04.3 LTS with kernel 4.4.0-98, llvm and clang of version 3.8 installed from packages, iproute2 is the latest from github.
$ tc qdisc add dev eth0 clsact
$ tc filter add dev eth0 ingress bpf \
object-file ./net-next.git/samples/bpf/parse_simple.o \
section simple verbose
Prog section 'simple' loaded (5)!
- Type: 3
- Instructions: 2 (0 over limit)
- License: GPL

source_code:
SEC("simple")
int handle_ingress(struct __sk_buff *skb)
{
return TC_ACT_SHOT;
}
So I want ANY packets to be dropped. I install it as follows:
This happens on Ubuntu 16.04.3 LTS with kernel 4.4.0-98, llvm and clang of version 3.8 installed from packages, iproute2 is the latest from github.
$ tc qdisc add dev eth0 clsact
$ tc filter add dev eth0 ingress bpf \
object-file ./net-next.git/samples/bpf/parse_simple.o \
section simple verbose
Prog section 'simple' loaded (5)!
- Type: 3
- Instructions: 2 (0 over limit)
- License: GPL
